title: The Discriminant and the Number of Solutions

blocks.body: The expression b^2 - 4ac, found under the radical in the quadratic formula. It tells you how many real solutions a quadratic equation has WITHOUT solving it.

If b^2 - 4ac > 0: TWO distinct real solutions (two x-intercepts on the graph). If b^2 - 4ac = 0: ONE real solution (the parabola just touches the x-axis). If b^2 - 4ac < 0: NO real solutions (parabola does not cross x-axis).

blocks.label: Example 2
blocks.body: x^2 - 6x + 9 = 0. Discriminant = 36 - 36 = 0. One real solution (x = 3, a double root).

blocks.label: Example 3
blocks.body: x^2 + x + 1 = 0. Discriminant = 1 - 4 = -3 < 0. No real solutions.

The discriminant is a powerful preview tool. When designing real-world problems, you can use the discriminant to determine whether the scenario has valid mathematical solutions before solving completely.
